title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two people discussing the popularity of 'true crime' podcasts.
extracts.audio_script: Woman: I’ve just finished that new podcast series about the 1920s cold case. I couldn't stop listening! Man: I find the whole 'true crime' craze a bit problematic, to be honest. [1]It often feels like these shows are exploiting real human tragedy for entertainment[/1]. Woman: I can see why you'd say that, but this one was very respectful. It actually helped raise money for the victim's family. Man: That’s a rarity, though. Most of them just focus on the 'gore' and the 'mystery' without considering the living relatives. Woman: [2]What I find fascinating is the psychological aspect—trying to understand what makes someone snap[/2]. It's not about the crime itself, but the human mind. Man: I suppose. I just wish there was more focus on prevention and the social causes of crime, rather than just the sensationalist details. Woman: Well, this series actually had a whole episode on the lack of social support at the time. You should give it a chance.

questions.question: The man's primary ethical concern about true crime podcasts is that they...

options.B: encourage people to commit similar crimes.
options.C: profit from the suffering of others.

questions.explanation: He says they are 'exploiting real human tragedy for entertainment', which means using someone's pain for gain/profit.

questions.question: What does the woman find most compelling about the genre?

options.A: The investigation of the perpetrator's motivations.
options.C: The historical details of the time periods.

questions.explanation: She mentions being fascinated by the 'psychological aspect' and 'understanding what makes someone snap'.

extracts.context: You hear two professionals discussing the merits of 'open-plan' offices.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I hear the bosses are planning to knock down the cubicles and go fully open-plan next month. Woman: Oh no. [3]I dread to think what that will do to my concentration levels[/3]. I can barely hear myself think as it is. Man: They say it’s supposed to 'foster collaboration' and 'spontaneous ideation'. Woman: That’s just corporate-speak for saving money on square footage. [4]In reality, everyone just wears noise-cancelling headphones and ignores each other anyway[/4]. Man: I actually enjoyed the open-plan layout at my last job. It made it much easier to catch people for a quick question without having to schedule a formal meeting. Woman: But surely it’s more disruptive? Every time someone has a 'quick question', everyone else in the vicinity is distracted. Man: There’s a balance to be struck, I suppose. But I'm looking forward to a bit more light and air in here.

questions.question: What is the woman's main fear about the new office layout?

options.A: A decrease in her personal workspace.
options.B: A negative impact on her productivity.
options.C: An increase in her daily workload.

questions.explanation: She says she 'dreads' what it will do to her 'concentration levels', which directly affects productivity.

questions.question: The woman believes that the 'collaboration' argument is...

options.B: an excuse for the company to reduce costs.
options.C: only effective in certain creative industries.

questions.explanation: She calls it 'corporate-speak for saving money on square footage', indicating she thinks the real motive is financial.

extracts.context: You hear two friends, a man and a woman, talking about an upcoming marathon.
extracts.audio_script: Man: So, only three weeks until the big race. How’s the training going? Woman: Honestly, [5]I think I’ve overdone it. My knee has been twinging every time I go over five miles[/5]. Man: That’s a worry. Have you seen a physio? Woman: I have. They’ve told me to rest, but the thought of losing all that progress is killing me. Man: Look, [6]it’s better to arrive at the start line slightly under-trained than injured[/6]. If you push it now, you might not be able to run at all. Woman: I know you're right. It’s just so hard to switch off that competitive mindset. Man: I get it. But remember why you’re doing this—it’s for the charity, not to set an Olympic record. Woman: True. I just don't want to let everyone down who's sponsored me. Man: They’ll be more disappointed if you're stuck on the sofa with a torn ligament!

questions.question: What problem is the woman currently facing?

options.A: She has lost motivation to continue training.
options.B: She is suffering from a physical ailment.
options.C: She hasn't had enough time to practice.

questions.explanation: She mentions her 'knee has been twinging', which is an informal way of describing a sharp, intermittent pain/injury.

questions.question: What advice does the man give the woman?

options.A: To increase the intensity of her runs gradually.
options.B: To focus on her mental preparation instead.
options.C: To prioritise her health over her training schedule.

questions.explanation: He says it's better to be 'under-trained than injured', advising her to stop pushing herself to avoid worsening the injury.
